skill.name: table-generator
skill.description: Automated generation of baseline characteristics tables (Table 1) for clinical research papers.
skill.summary: Automated generation of baseline characteristics tables (Table 1) for clinical research papers.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 52 potential issues, all of which are false positives after evaluation. Backtick execution detections correspond to markdown code fences in documentation, not shell command substitution. Weak crypto detections are false matches on non-cryptographic text. Path traversal detections reference a security checklist that documents prevention measures. The Python script (scripts/main.py) is a standard data analysis tool with no network calls, no dynamic code execution, and no environment variable access. An independent prior audit confirms PASS on all security gates with an overall score of 83/100 and a deployable rating of Limited Release.

content.user_title: Generate clinical research Table 1 with automated statistics
content.value_statement: Clinical researchers spend hours manually creating baseline characteristics tables. This skill automates Table 1 generation with proper statistical tests and publication-ready formatting.
actual_capabilities: Generate complete baseline characteristics tables from patient CSV data
Automatically detect continuous and categorical variable types
Compute appropriate statistics: mean with SD, median with IQR, counts with percentages
Perform group comparisons using t-tests, ANOVA, and chi-square tests
Output formatted tables in CSV format for publication and further editing
Report statistical test results with p-values for each variable
limitations: Requires CSV input with properly formatted column headers
Does not handle survival analysis, regression models, or adjusted comparisons
Limited to standard parametric tests; no non-parametric alternatives available
Output formatting is basic CSV; no direct Word, PDF, or LaTeX export

use_cases.title: Manuscript preparation for medical journal
use_cases.description: A clinical researcher imports patient data from a completed trial and generates a publication-ready Table 1 with proper group comparisons and p-values for the results section of a journal manuscript.

use_cases.title: Study report for research project
use_cases.description: A medical student or research fellow creates a baseline characteristics table for a study report, including automated variable type detection and appropriate summary statistics.

use_cases.title: Exploratory analysis of clinical trial data
use_cases.description: A biostatistician performs rapid exploratory analysis on trial data to assess baseline balance between treatment and control groups before proceeding with more complex modeling.

prompt_templates.title: Basic table generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Table 1 from patients.csv with all variables summarized.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user with a simple dataset and no grouping variable

prompt_templates.title: Table with group comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Table 1 from patients.csv. Use treatment as the grouping variable and include p-values comparing the groups.
prompt_templates.scenario: User comparing treatment and control groups in a clinical trial

prompt_templates.title: Table with selected variables
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a Table 1 from patients.csv grouped by treatment_group. Only include the following variables: age, bmi, blood_pressure, cholesterol, smoking_status. Save the output to table1_output.csv.
prompt_templates.scenario: User with a large dataset who wants to select specific variables

prompt_templates.title: Full pipeline with validation
prompt_templates.prompt: First, validate that patients.csv exists and contains the expected columns (age, gender, bmi, treatment). Then generate a complete Table 1 with the treatment variable as the grouping column. Include missing data counts and save the result to results/table1_final.csv.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert user running a reproducible data analysis pipeline

output_examples.input: Generate a baseline characteristics table from patients.csv with treatment as the grouping variable.
output_examples.output: The skill produces a formatted table showing each variable, its type, summary statistics per group, and statistical test results. For example: Age (Continuous) shows mean +/- SD for each group with a t-test p-value. Gender (Categorical) shows count and percentage for each category with a chi-square p-value. Missing data counts are included where applicable.

output_examples.input: Generate a Table 1 from clinical_trial_data.csv with all variables. No grouping needed.
output_examples.output: The skill produces a baseline table for the full cohort. Continuous variables display mean +/- SD and median [IQR]. Categorical variables display n (%). Each variable is automatically classified as continuous or categorical based on its data type and unique value count.

best_practices: Clean your dataset before generating the table: handle missing values, standardize column names, and verify that categorical variables are properly encoded.
Always specify the grouping variable when comparing cohorts; this enables automatic statistical testing and produces publication-relevant p-values.
Review the auto-detected variable types and adjust if needed; the heuristic may misclassify ordinal variables with few unique values.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for survival analysis, regression modeling, or any analysis that requires adjusted comparisons or confounding control.
Do not include variables with free-text or unstructured data; the script expects clean categorical or numeric columns.
Do not skip input validation; always confirm the CSV file exists and contains the expected column names before running the script.

faq.question: What file format does the input data need to be in?
faq.answer: The input must be a CSV file with column headers. Each row should represent one patient or observation. Numeric columns should contain numbers, and categorical columns should contain discrete labels.

faq.question: What statistical tests does this skill use?
faq.answer: For two-group comparisons, it uses an independent t-test for continuous variables. For three or more groups, it uses ANOVA. For categorical variables, it uses a chi-square test of independence.

faq.question: Can I select which variables to include in the table?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the --vars parameter to specify a comma-separated list of column names. If no variables are specified, all columns except the grouping variable are included.

faq.question: Does this skill handle missing data?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill drops missing values within each variable before computing statistics. P-values and descriptive statistics are calculated only on available data.

faq.question: Can I export the table to Word or PDF?
faq.answer: No. The output is saved as a CSV file. You can import the CSV into Word, Google Docs, or a spreadsheet application for further formatting.

faq.question: Is this skill suitable for regulatory submissions?
faq.answer: This skill is designed for exploratory analysis and manuscript preparation. For regulatory submissions, you should validate all results with certified statistical software and follow applicable regulatory guidelines.
